content.assessment: As a no-new-policy counterfactual, this proposal requires no coalition-building or legislative action at all and is politically costless to sustain in the sense that it involves no active decision exposed to organized resistance. Its political character is therefore one of default continuation rather than achieved reform, meaning it inherits and preserves the unaddressed political dynamics (director lobbying access, church-sector growth, elite demand for selective tracks) that currently sustain segregation, without providing any lever to contest them.
opportunities: Could serve as a politically low-risk baseline against which any future active reform could be benchmarked without prematurely triggering opposition mobilization
strengths: Requires no legislative or administrative action, making it immune to legislative veto players or coalition-formation requirements by construction
Already observably underway (application shifts, capacity stagnation), meaning it carries no additional implementation or enactment risk beyond what already exists
threats: Uneven geographic impact could create new politically salient grievances (rural collapse vs. urban persistence) that mobilize opposition to *any* subsequent reform attempt, including favorable ones

Because church-sector growth and Roma segregation are unaddressed, passive contraction could be publicly framed as government inaction, creating legitimacy risk distinct from the risks facing active proposals
weaknesses: Provides no mechanism to address the ideologically entrenched pro-stratification policy stance of the governing coalition or the political access enjoyed by director interest groups, leaving those dynamics fully intact

Passive and uncoordinated nature means politically salient disruptions (rural school closures, settlement-viability crises) could occur without any governance structure to manage public legitimacy concerns
